title: SEGMENT 12 — TAPS — TET-assisted pyridine borane sequencing, the gentler successor

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — TAPS — TET-assisted pyridine borane sequencing, the gentler successor

The first of the bisulfite-free, base-resolution methylation methods to make a serious commercial impact was TAPS — TET-Assisted Pyridine borane Sequencing, published in 2019 in Nature Biotechnology by Yibin Liu and Chunxiao Song at the University of Oxford.

The paper, titled "Bisulfite-free direct detection of 5-methylcytosine and 5-hydroxymethylcytosine at base resolution," made a clean break with bisulfite while preserving everything that made bisulfite scientifically attractive — base resolution, genome-wide coverage, quantitative methylation levels.
The chemistry of TAPS is conceptually inverse to bisulfite.
Bisulfite converts unmethylated C to T and leaves methylated C intact, so methylated Cs are read as Cs.
TAPS, by contrast, converts methylated C to T and leaves unmethylated C intact, so the methylated Cs are the ones that show up as a sequence change in the reads.
Here's the mechanism in three steps.
Step one: TET enzymatic oxidation.
A TET enzyme — the same TET that does demethylation in the cell — is applied in vitro to oxidize 5mC and 5hmC all the way to 5-carboxylcytosine (5caC).
Both 5mC and 5hmC end up as 5caC after this step; unmethylated C is not touched.
Step two: pyridine borane reduction.
A mild chemical reducing agent, pyridine borane, reduces 5caC to dihydrouracil (DHU).
The intermediate 5caC, with its carboxyl group, is uniquely susceptible to this reduction; unmodified C is not.
Step three: standard PCR.
The polymerase reads DHU as T.
So at every position that started as 5mC or 5hmC, you now read T; at every position that started as unmethylated C, you still read C.
The advantages over bisulfite are substantial.
First, the chemistry is gentle.
Pyridine borane reduction happens at neutral pH and modest temperature.
There's no extreme acidity, no extended high-temperature incubation, no extensive depurination.
DNA recovery is dramatically better — typically over 90 percent of starting DNA survives, compared to bisulfite's 10 to 30 percent.
Second, longer reads are possible.
Because the DNA is intact, you can do long-fragment libraries — Pacific Biosciences, Oxford Nanopore, or long-fragment Illumina — that you simply cannot do well with bisulfite-damaged DNA.
Third, the alphabet is preserved.
Most Cs in the genome are unmethylated, and TAPS leaves those unmethylated Cs as Cs.
The reads still have a four-letter alphabet, alignment is much easier, mapping rates approach those of untreated Illumina sequencing.
There's one trade-off.

TAPS reads 5mC plus 5hmC together — the TET step oxidizes both — so to discriminate the two marks you need a variant called TAPSβ or chemical-assisted TAPS that protects 5hmC by chemical β-glucosylation before the TET step.
So discrimination is possible but requires the extra step, much as it does for oxBS or TAB-seq.
Here is the analogy.
TAPS is the gentler replacement for bisulfite.
Same single-base resolution, same genome-wide coverage, same quantitative methylation, but the chemistry uses an enzyme and a mild reducing agent instead of harsh acid at high temperature.
DNA stays largely intact, libraries are higher-quality, you can do long reads, and the read alphabet is preserved.
It's bisulfite's better-engineered successor.

TAPS has been adopted relatively slowly, partly because the chemistry was patented and the kits were initially expensive, and partly because the bisulfite-based analytical infrastructure was so entrenched that switching cost was non-trivial.
But for high-value applications where input is precious or read length matters — particularly liquid biopsy and ancient DNA — TAPS and TAPS-like chemistry have started to displace bisulfite.

The company Cambridge Epigenetix, founded around the Balasubramanian lab's earlier work and later acquired by biomodal, commercialized TAPS-related technology, including a method called duet evoC that produces methylation, hydroxymethylation, and genetic variant calls simultaneously from a single library prep.

The TAPS story is an example of how, once a foundational technology has been around long enough, the field's accumulated knowledge of its limitations sets up the demand for a better alternative, and someone — often a chemist who specifically thinks about base modifications — figures out the better chemistry.
The 5mC discrimination, the input requirement, the read length — all of these were known problems with bisulfite, and TAPS attacks all of them in one chemical move.
